narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 76% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Looking at your profile, you ranked in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—which signals early and ongoing academic struggles. Your LSAT score of 19 is also well under the average of around 37 that successful students usually bring, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 lags behind the standard 3.2. Coming from a middle-income family, identifying as male and Asian, and planning part-time work put you in less common but not drastically offbeat categories compared to most applicants, who are overwhelmingly full-time white students from varied backgrounds.

The model's decision hinged most heavily on your third-year decile rank, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward bar failure. Right behind that was your low LSAT score, followed closely by your first-year decile, each amplifying concerns about your readiness. Your middle family income level, Asian racial background, and undergraduate GPA played supporting roles in tipping the scales further toward failure, with your part-time plans and male gender having the smallest but still unfavorable impacts. Unlike typical students who pass the bar—who boast stronger early grades, higher test scores, and full-time focus—your metrics across the board fell short, creating a consistent pattern the model couldn't ignore.

To turn this around for future applications or bar prep, prioritize rebuilding your academic foundation: retake the LSAT with targeted practice to aim closer to average scores, since it's one area you can directly improve. Develop study habits that could have lifted your school deciles, like consistent routines proven effective for similar students. While family income, background, gender, and work status are harder to shift, excelling in controllable areas like test prep and GPA-boosting coursework elsewhere could reshape your profile enough to sway predictions next time. Many students in tough spots have succeeded by focusing here— you've got the chance to rewrite your story.
